1:
The witnesses open the chapter on a scene of horror and read it, almost with one voice, as the moment the promise to David hung by a single thread. The grammatical-historical Ellicott does the close work: the opening and before the verb is a mistaken repetition of the last letter of Ahaziah's name, so the sense is 'Now Athaliah had seen.' He fixes her identity precisely — daughter of Ahab, granddaughter of Omri, wife of Jehoram, and now, by her ambition and cruelty, 'a worthy daughter of Jezebel.' The seed royal, he adds, means all who might set up a claim to the succession; Ahaziah's brothers had already been cut off by the Arabs (2 Chron. 21:17) and his kinsmen by Jehu (2 Kings 10:14), so those Athaliah destroyed would be chiefly Ahaziah's own sons.

The puritan Matthew Henry lifts the massacre into the light of covenant. God had ordained a lamp for his anointed (Ps. 132:17), and here that lamp is 'almost extinguished and yet wonderfully preserved.' He weighs Athaliah's motives twofold: a spirit of ambition, that none might reign with her, so that she slew 'even the infants and sucklings'; and a spirit of revenge against God, resolving, now that the house of Ahab was destroyed, to cut off the house of David in defiance of God's word — 'a foolish attempt and fruitless, for who can disannul what God hath purposed?' He marvels at the unnatural crime, that one who had herself been a king's daughter, wife, and mother should be the wilful murderer of her own grandchildren.

The Wesleyan voices press the lesson home. Clarke cries, 'How dreadful is the lust of reigning! it destroys all the charities of life,' turning fathers and mothers into savages, and warns the reader against the seas of blood through which men swim to sovereign rule. Wesley traces the disaster to its root: this was the bitter fruit of Jehoshaphat's marrying his son into the idolatrous house of Ahab, a judgment God permitted 'to shew how much he abhors all such affinities.' The voices do not divide; where Ellicott and Gill see Jezebel's temper reproduced and Henry the rage of a doomed house, all confess the same wonder — that against a queen's sword and a mother's malice the covenant with David could not be broken.
2:
Against the darkness of the first verse the witnesses set a single act of rescue, and they attend first to the woman who dares it. The Reformed Gill and the Wesleyan Clarke agree on a delicate point: Jehosheba was daughter of King Joram, but not by Athaliah, for a high priest 'would not have married the daughter of such an idolatrous woman.' Clarke supplies the genealogy — she was sister to Ahaziah but by a different mother, since Joash's own mother was Zibiah of Beersheba (2 Kings 12:1). The grammatical-historical Ellicott draws out what the marriage itself declares: that a king's sister should be wife to the high priest shows 'what almost royal dignity belonged to the high priest's office.'

Ellicott and Keil then do the textual labour the story needs. Ellicott reads 'the king's sons which were slain' more exactly as 'which were to be put to death' — Jehosheba stole the infant away at the very moment the order was given, and his infancy caused him to be overlooked; the clause 'and they hid him' has slipped out of place, and a verb 'and she put' has fallen out, so that the true run is 'she hid him and his nurse in the chamber of the beds.' That chamber, he explains, was the store-room where the mattresses and coverlets were kept, an unoccupied room that made the nearest hiding-place before the babe could be conveyed secretly within the Temple. Keil, comparing the parallel in 2 Chronicles 23, notes that Kings and Chronicles are independent extracts from one fuller original, which accounts for their apparent discrepancies.

The Wesleyan Wesley gathers the theology. The hiding was the work of Jehosheba and her husband Jehoiada, and the bedchamber lay 'in the house of the Lord,' one of the chambers reserved for the priests and Levites, which made it the fitter refuge. Here he echoes the great theme: 'Now was the promise made to David bound up in one life. And yet it did not fail.' He marvels at the providence that a wicked king had married his daughter to a holy priest — a match some thought a disparagement to the royal house, yet it proved its salvation, since Jehoiada's interest in the Temple gave her the means to preserve the child, and her interest in the palace gave him the means, in time, to set the child upon the throne. The voices land together: God will always raise up protectors for those whom he means to protect.
3:
The single verse that covers six years is read by the witnesses as the long, silent hinge of the whole deliverance — the rightful king alive but buried, the usurper enthroned above him. The Reformed Gill locates the refuge with care: not, as Jarchi imagined, in the holy of holies, but, with Kimchi, in one of the chambers belonging to the priests and Levites, of which the high priest had one for his own use. He reckons the arithmetic that the narrative leaves implied — the child was but a year old when he was taken and hidden, so that he was seven when at last he was shown and crowned.

The Wesleyan Clarke explains why so bold a concealment could succeed: it 'might be readily done, because none had access to the temple but the priests; and the high priest himself was the chief manager of this business.' The safest place in the kingdom for David's heir was the house of David's God, precisely where the idolatrous queen was least likely to come. The grammatical-historical Ellicott adds the fine points of the Hebrew: the boy was 'with her' — with Jehosheba his aunt — 'in the house of the Lord,' perhaps even in the high priest's residence within the sacred precincts; and the closing clause is better rendered 'Athaliah was reigning over the land,' a continuous, settled tyranny. Against the critic Thenius, who would make Jehosheba no more than a fugitive sharing the child's asylum and her marriage to the high priest a mere 'traditional interpretation,' Ellicott insists that the chronicler 'had better authority than mere tradition' for what he records.

The verse is thin in comment but heavy in meaning, and the witnesses feel its weight. For six years the promise to David is reduced to a hidden infant while a daughter of Ahab wears the crown; the throne of Judah is usurped, and the true king is a secret nursed in the Temple. Yet the very quietness of the notice is its comfort: what God has purposed cannot be undone by six years of Baal and blood. The hidden child in the house of the Lord is the pledge that the line which runs to the Son of David (compare 2 Kings 12:1) will not be extinguished, however long it must wait in the dark.
4:
With the seventh year the story breaks its long silence, and the witnesses gather around the man who breaks it. The puritan Matthew Henry gives the moment its theology: through six years of tyranny 'Joash lay hid, entitled to a crown and intended for it, and yet buried alive in obscurity,' so that 'though the sons and heirs of heaven are now hidden, the world knows them not (1 John 3:1), yet the time is fixed when they shall appear in glory, as Joash in his seventh year.' He paints Jehoiada as the fit deliverer — by office a man whom the people were bound to obey (Deut. 17:12), by marriage allied to the royal house, and one who, we may believe, did not attempt this until he had 'asked counsel of God.' His management was all discretion: he concerted the matter with the rulers of hundreds, bound them by an oath of secrecy, and only then 'showed them the king's son.'

The Wesleyan Clarke reads the tactics the same way — Jehoiada 'had certainly sounded them all' beforehand, and now brings the chief officers into the Temple to bind them by oath and reveal the heir 'in whose behalf they are to rise.' The Reformed Gill supplies the institutional frame: these rulers of hundreds belong to the priests and Levites, whom the high priest could lawfully convene without suspicion, drawn from the twenty-four courses that David had appointed.

Here the grammatical-historical commentators open the chapter's running dispute. Ellicott and Keil render the muster not as Levites but as 'the centurions of the Carians and the Couriers' — the officers commanding the royal body-guard. Keil notes that Chronicles fills out the scene: the captains went through the land and summoned the Levites and heads of families to Jerusalem, and there Jehoiada made his covenant with them. Ellicott observes that Jehoiada's rank as high priest is oddly unstated on his first mention, a sign that the original source had named him fully at the outset (as in 2 Chronicles 23), and he defends the plausibility of a foreign mercenary guard by pointing to David's own service among the Philistines and to Uriah the Hittite in his ranks. So the first division appears and will recur: are 'the rulers over hundreds' commanders of priestly Levites (Gill, Wesley) or centurions of the king's guard (Ellicott, Keil)? On the substance all agree — the discontent had reached its climax, and the high priest, prayerful and prudent, now bound armed men by oath to set the son of David on his throne.
5:
Jehoiada's first order of battle is a study in method, and the witnesses divide over who receives it while agreeing on what it achieves. The Reformed Gill takes the men addressed to be rulers of hundreds among the priests and Levites, and unfolds the system of David's twenty-four courses: the company entering upon the Sabbath and the company going off that day were both at hand, so that a double force was available for the enterprise. The Wesleyan voices press the same reading. Wesley glosses 'of you' as the Levites of the courses, 'enter in' as coming into the Temple to minister, and 'the king's house' as that quarter which led toward the palace 'which Athaliah now possessed.' Clarke adds the shrewd reason for choosing the Sabbath: it was 'a day of public concourse,' so that the gathering of the conspirators 'would not be noticed,' and the men, coming unarmed, could be quietly equipped from the Temple's stores (v. 10).

The grammatical-historical commentators read the same dispositions but assign them to soldiers, not Levites. Ellicott explains that the royal guard 'regularly succeeded each other on duty just as they do in modern European capitals,' the Sabbath being their relief-day, as it was also for the priestly guilds whose organisation so resembled an army's. He draws out the strategy's geography: two places had to be secured at once — 'the king's house,' the palace where the usurping queen sat and whose adherents must be shut out, and 'the house of the Lord,' where the young heir must be shielded from attack. Keil, working the difficult Hebrew, notes a corrupted verb and reconstructs the sense: a third of you keep the watch of the king's house, a third at the gate Sur, a third at the gate behind the runners.

So the recurring debate over the guard sounds again — Levites (Gill, Wesley) or the king's runners (Ellicott, Keil) — yet the design is undisputed and admirable. The forces are apportioned so that no gate is unwatched and no quarter left open, and the Sabbath's doubled manpower is turned to a single end. Even in a coup Jehoiada moves with the order of the sanctuary, dividing the men to their stations before ever the king is shown.
6:
This verse plants the guard at particular doors, and the witnesses turn topographer, tracing gates whose very names are contested. The Reformed Gill reports the Jewish tradition that the gate of Sur was the eastern gate, called also 'the gate of the foundation' because there the sanctuary's foundation was first laid, and where the defiled were bidden to turn back — a gate that the rabbis said bore no fewer than six names. The Wesleyan Wesley takes Sur simply as 'the chief gate of the temple' and 'the guard' as the royal or the temple guard on the south side, and sums the intent: 'you shall guard all the gates or entrances into the temple, that neither Athaliah nor any of her soldiers may break in.'

The grammatical-historical commentators sharpen every word. Ellicott notes that where our text reads Sur ('turning aside'), Chronicles reads Jesod ('foundation'), and judges the two either alternative names for one side-door or, more likely, a textual corruption of the one into the other; he pictures the gate as a side exit from the palace court such as survives in the old Egyptian palaces at Medinet-Habu and Karnak. The 'gate behind the guard,' he adds, is the gate of the Couriers named later (v. 19), the principal entrance to the palace, the word 'behind' perhaps a mere scribal echo. Both he and Keil wrestle with the closing word, a term found nowhere else in Scripture: Keil renders 'keep guard over the house for defence,' while Ellicott takes it as a marginal gloss meaning 'repulse' — the guards were to 'keep back any one who tried to enter the palace buildings,' as Gesenius paraphrased, 'to drive off the people.'

Beneath the tangle of Sur and Jesod, palace-gate and temple-gate, the design is plain, and the witnesses see it alike: every avenue is stopped so that neither the queen's escape nor her rescue is possible, and the holy house is fenced against any rush of the crowd. The care that names each gate is the same care that will not have God's house profaned even in the hour of revolution.
7:
The verse turns from the perimeter to the person, and though only a few witnesses stop over it, they draw out its quiet ingenuity. The Reformed Gill notes that the two companies here are the course whose week of service ended with the Sabbath; instead of being dismissed to the country, they were 'detained for the present purpose, and divided into two parts.' The Wesleyan Wesley says the same and points to the fuller record in 2 Chronicles 23:8: these men, 'who having finished their course, should have gone home, but were detained,' were now set with a special charge — 'while the rest guard the entrances into the temple, these shall have a special care of the king's person.'

The puritan Matthew Henry supplies the mechanism the others assume. David had divided the priests into courses that changed each Sabbath, so that on that day, when double service was to be done, 'there was a double number to do it, both those that were to come in and those that were to go out.' Jehoiada seized precisely this doubling: the incoming company mans the gates, and the outgoing company, kept back from its rest, is massed 'round about the king.' The arrangement is not two-thirds against one-third of a single watch by accident but the deliberate use of the Sabbath's overlapping shifts, so that the whole of one company can be spared for the guarding of the boy.

The verse repays the reader who lingers, for it shows the shape of the whole plan in miniature. The larger body is placed not at the doors but at the center, its weapons turned inward toward the one life on which the covenant hangs. As at the perimeter the design keeps out the usurper, so at the heart it hedges the king; the concentric care that stops the gates concentrates its strength upon the person of the Lord's anointed. What the outer companies do for the house, the two inner parts do for its rightful heir — they surround him, so that the promise made to David, still bound up in one small life, is ringed with drawn swords and cannot be reached.
8:
The order reaches its sharp point: a ring of armed men about the king, and death to any who break the ring. The witnesses are few here, and they divide chiefly over one word — the 'ranges' within which the intruder falls. The Reformed Gill reads the command plainly: 'ye shall compass the king round about, every man with his weapons in his hand,' the weapons being those presently to be given out from the Temple stores (v. 10). The grammatical-historical Keil renders the whole with military precision: 'whoever presses into the ranks shall be slain, and shall be with the king when he goes out and in' — that is, the guard is to keep its formation unbroken and to attend the king in all his steps.

The Wesleyan Wesley, however, takes 'the ranges' not of the ranks of soldiers but of the stonework, glossing the word as 'fences, the wall wherewith the courts of the temple were environed.' So a genuine, if small, division opens: for Keil the deadly boundary is the living cordon of the guard, into which no man may force his way; for Wesley it is the enclosing wall of the courts, past which no intruder may pass. Either way the effect is one — a fixed limit, guarded on pain of death, drawn about the person of the king.

Brief as it is, the verse completes the picture the previous orders began. The young king is to be not merely watched but encircled, every weapon turned toward whoever would come near, and the guard is bound to him so closely that they move as he moves, 'as he goeth out and as he cometh in.' The child who for six years was hidden in secret is now hedged in the open by a wall of steel; the same providence that concealed him now surrounds him. The order that any who press within the cordon shall die is not cruelty but the measure of what is at stake, for within that ring stands the last life of the house of David, and with him the whole promise of God.
9:
The narrative turns from command to execution, and the witnesses mark the prompt, exact obedience with which the plan is carried out. The Reformed Gill records simply that 'the captains over the hundreds did according to all things that Jehoiada the priest commanded,' each taking his men — both the company coming in on the Sabbath and the company going out — and bringing them to the priest. The grammatical-historical commentators add their precisions. Ellicott identifies the captains as the centurions of the royal guard named already (v. 4) and corrects the tense to 'all that Jehoiada had commanded,' so that the verse looks back over the completed orders. Keil, reading the same block, stresses that they mustered exactly as directed, joining the incoming and outgoing companies and presenting themselves to Jehoiada for arming.

The puritan Matthew Henry draws the lesson that the bare report conceals. Jehoiada 'appointed to each his place as well as his work, for good order contributes very much to the expediting and accomplishing of any great enterprise.' The strength of the moment lies not in numbers but in coordination: 'let every man know, and keep, and make good, his post, and then the work will be done quickly.' The obedience of the captains, echoing back the high priest's command word for word, is itself the hinge of the deliverance.

The verse also lets the chapter's running question surface once more — whether these captains command Levites (as Gill holds) or the king's own guard (as Ellicott and Keil maintain) — but it is a difference of identity, not of conduct, for on the conduct all are agreed. What Jehoiada devised in secret is now set in motion in the open, and it moves without a flaw because every man does precisely what he was told. Nothing is improvised, and nothing is left to the temper of the crowd; the whole enterprise rests on orders given and orders kept. The deliverance of David's house comes not by tumult but by discipline: a wise command met by an unhesitating obedience, each in his appointed station, so that when the king is at last brought forth, the whole apparatus of protection is already in place and waiting. The witnesses, differing over who the captains were, are one in praising how they obeyed.
10:
Before the king can be shown, the unarmed men must be armed, and the witnesses dwell with one accord on the fitness of the weapons chosen. The Reformed Gill explains that the spears and shields Jehoiada distributed were those 'King David's' — taken in war from his enemies, dedicated to the service of the Temple, and long laid up there; given now to the captains, they armed men who had come in unarmed and unsuspected, so that they could be equipped 'without being seen and known.' The Wesleyan Clarke confirms the detail from Josephus, who says expressly that Jehoiada 'opened the arsenal in the temple, which David had prepared,' and shared out among the centurions, priests, and Levites the spears, quivers, and every other weapon he found there.

The grammatical-historical commentators refine the reading. Ellicott notes that the Hebrew has the singular 'spear' where Chronicles has the plural, and that 'shields' may rather be 'arms' or 'weapons' in general (compare 2 Chronicles 23:9); Bähr's suggestion, that the guards coming off palace duty had left their own weapons behind, he sets beside the older view. Keil describes the result: 'the guard stood, every man with his weapons in his hand, round about the king,' ranged from the right corner of the Temple to the left, along by the altar and the house, a wall of David's arms about David's heir.

What the witnesses feel in common is the poetry of the providence. The very weapons with which David had once won his kingdom, hung up as trophies in the house of God and gathering the dust of years, are now taken down to set David's descendant on David's throne. Ellicott catches it exactly: the arms 'which David had laid up in the Temple as spoils of war were now to be used, appropriately enough, for the restoration of David's heir.' Gill adds the practical wisdom of it — the men could be armed from this hidden store 'without being seen and known,' so that the show of force came only when the king was ready to be shown, and not before. The sanctuary that had sheltered the child now furnishes the steel that will crown him, and the founder's own spears defend the founder's imperilled promise. What David had dedicated to God, God now returns to the keeping of David's house.
11:
The dispositions complete, the witnesses pause on the tableau itself: a living wall of armed men standing between the queen's world and the king. The Reformed Gill states it plainly — 'the guard stood, every man with his weapons in his hand, round about the king,' exactly as they had been ordered. The grammatical-historical commentators bring the scene into focus. Keil describes the two flanks reaching 'from the right corner of the temple to the left corner, along by the altar and the temple,' the guard drawn up in the open court about the boy.

Ellicott presses the Hebrew for the picture's precise geometry. 'The guard' here, he insists, is literally 'the Couriers,' the royal runners, 'not therefore the Levites' — the chapter's standing question sounding once more. He corrects the English: read 'side' for 'corner' and 'at' for 'along by,' so that the men 'formed in two lines, extending from the south wall to the north wall of the court, one line standing at the altar of burnt offering, which was near the entrance, the other at the sanctuary itself.' The phrase 'round about the king,' he notes, is anticipative — the encircling comes fully only when the prince is led out into the guarded space between the ranks.

The verse is a hinge between preparation and proclamation, and its power is in the image it fixes. Between the altar and the holy house, in the very court where God is worshipped, the last son of David stands hedged by a double line of drawn weapons. The armed men are the visible form of an invisible keeping: what God had done in secret for six years, hiding the child in a Temple chamber, he now does in the open, ringing him with steel in the Temple court. Every spear points outward from the king; the whole scene declares that the Lord's anointed, so long concealed, is now both revealed and defended, and that the promise which had shrunk to one hidden life is about to be crowned in the sight of all. Whether the men be royal Couriers or Levites of the courses, they stand for the same truth: the throne of David is not defended by the child's own might but by the guard that God has raised about him.
12:
Now the hidden heir is crowned, and the witnesses read the four acts of the coronation as the very pattern of kingship under God. The Reformed Gill notes only that Jehoiada 'brought forth the king's son' from the chamber where he had been reared, but the grammatical-historical Keil lays out the sequence: he set the crown upon him and gave him 'the testimony,' the book of the law, as the rule of his life and reign according to the command of Deut. 17:18. The Wesleyan Clarke describes the regalia — the crown a golden diadem, the testimony 'the book of the law, written on a roll,' which was in effect his sceptre — and observes that diadem, testimony, and anointing oil together were essential to a lawful consecration; he marks too that this is the first instance on record of clapping the hands for joy, and renders the people's cry 'God save the king' exactly, 'May the king live!' Wesley presses why the law is called a 'testimony': it is 'a witness of God's will, and man's duty,' put into the king's hand to remind him at his entrance that he must govern himself and his kingdom by it.

The puritan Matthew Henry gathers the whole into worship. He hears the prophet's summons, 'Rejoice greatly, O daughter of Zion... thy king appears' (Zech. 9:9), and reads each act as a symbol: the crown for kingly power, the testimony for the rule of law — 'he gave him the testimony, put into his hand a Bible, in which he must read all the days of his life' — the anointing for the gift of the Spirit, and the clapping and acclamation for the people's glad consent. The anointing especially he takes as typical, for the kings of David's house were anointed as figures of Christ, 'anointed above his fellows'; and he turns it pastorally: with such joy 'must the kingdom of Christ be welcomed into our hearts when his throne is set up there and Satan the usurper is deposed.'

One real difference of reading remains, over 'the testimony.' Most — Gill, Keil, Clarke, Wesley, and Henry — take it for the book of the law, the covenant-charter laid on the new king. But Ellicott records the rabbis' dissent: Kimchi understood it of a royal robe, and others of a phylactery worn on the coronet (compare Deut. 6:8); he also defends the chronicler's note that 'Jehoiada and his sons' performed the anointing. Whether the testimony be a scroll or an insignia, the witnesses agree on its meaning: the boy is crowned not to his own will but under God's word, and the shout that greets him is the joy of a people receiving back their rightful king.
13:
The shout of the coronation reaches the palace, and the usurper walks straight into her ruin. The Reformed Gill sets the scene: Athaliah heard 'the acclamations and shouts at the coronation,' for by now the people had learned what was afoot and came flocking to see and praise the new king. The grammatical-historical Keil pictures her arrival — drawn by the noise, she came to the people into the house of the Lord, and there beheld the young king in his appointed place, ringed by the captains and the trumpeters, the whole people rejoicing and blowing trumpets, whereupon she rent her clothes and cried 'Conspiracy!' He notes the small textual repair that the crowd was 'the people and the halberdiers,' a dropped letter obscuring the guard. Ellicott, restoring the same lost 'and,' observes what the verse quietly implies — that Athaliah could come so quickly 'into the temple' shows the palace stood hard by the house of God.

The puritan Matthew Henry reads the moment as tragic irony and divine justice. Like her mother Jezebel before her, Athaliah 'saved them the labour' — she went out to meet her judges and so 'hastened her own destruction.' That she came at all, and seemingly alone, and only when the shout was already in her ears, shows how 'little regarded' she had become; had the plot been discovered sooner it might have been crushed, but now it was too late. Henry finds the hand of God in her folly: 'Whom God will destroy he befools.' And he fastens on the cry itself: she who was 'the greatest traitor' was yet 'first and loudest in crying Treason! treason!' — a standing proof that 'those that are themselves most guilty are commonly most forward to reproach others.'

The witnesses do not divide here; they read the verse as the turning of the tables. The queen who six years before had waded through the blood of princes now finds a prince enthroned in the sanctuary she could not enter, and her rent robes and false alarm are the first signs of a doom she has run to meet. The noise that terrifies her is the joy of a delivered people, and her frantic charge of treason only names her own crime aloud. The stage is set, by her own coming, for the justice that the next verses will execute.
14:
What Athaliah sees undoes her, and the witnesses linger over the picture and dispute the king's exact posture. The Reformed Gill reports that 'the king stood by a pillar, as the manner was' of the kings of David's house when they came into the Temple, and canvasses the traditions of where that place was — Solomon's brazen scaffold, the post of the east gate, or a royal throne on a marble pillar near the upper gate 'where the kings of the house of David sat' — concluding only that Athaliah saw Joash 'with the crown on his head, and in the place where kings used to sit or stand.' The grammatical-historical Keil fills the tableau: the princes and the trumpeters by the king, all the people of the land rejoicing and blowing trumpets, and Athaliah rending her clothes with the cry, 'Treason, treason.'

Here the commentators divide over a preposition. The Wesleyan Clarke reads that the king 'stood ON a pillar or tribunal, the place or throne on which they were accustomed to put the kings when they proclaimed them,' and derives the queen's cry from a root meaning 'to bind, unite together' — a conspiracy. Ellicott agrees against the plain KJV: the king 'was standing on the stand,' a dais reserved for the king before the great altar (compare 2 Chron. 23:13), not merely beside a temple pillar. So a genuine difference of reading appears — the king beside a pillar (Gill, Keil) or upon a raised platform (Clarke, Ellicott) — though both agree it was the customary royal station.

Ellicott adds two further touches of learning. The 'princes' are the chiefs of the people, not the centurions of the guard who have their own name throughout; the Greek and Latin versions, reading a slightly different word, render 'singers.' And 'the trumpeters' is literally 'the trumpets,' the sacred priestly clarions (compare Num. 10:2), whose sounding proves that the priests and Levites were present in force, exactly as Chronicles represents. The verse, on any reading, fixes the fatal contrast: the lawful king crowned and acclaimed in his own place, the usurping queen with her garments torn, crying treason against the very justice that has caught her.
15:
Jehoiada's order for the queen's removal is read by the witnesses as a marvel of reverence in the very act of judgment. The Reformed Gill takes 'the captains of the hundreds, the officers of the host' to be the commanders of the priests and Levites, and the Wesleyan Wesley agrees, glossing 'the host' as 'these companies of Levites, who are elsewhere called the Lord's host, and now were the king's host.' The Wesleyan Clarke explains the situation: Athaliah 'had pressed in among the guards into the temple,' and the order concerning 'him that followeth' means that whoever took her part 'let him instantly be slain.'

The grammatical-historical Ellicott sharpens each clause. The captains are the centurions of the royal guard, called 'the officers of the host' (compare Num. 31:14) because it was they who gave effect to the high priest's orders by passing them to their troops. 'Have her forth without the ranges' he renders 'cause her to go out between the ranks' — she was to be escorted out of the sacred precincts with a guard on either side; and 'him that followeth her' means any who showed her sympathy, since her partisans might well be scattered through the great crowd. The parenthesis 'for the priest had said' he treats as an explanatory note, and 'had said' as perhaps meaning 'thought.'

The puritan Matthew Henry draws out the governing motive, which all the witnesses share: the care 'that she should not be killed in the temple, or any of the courts of it, in reverence to that holy place, which must not be stained with the blood of any human sacrifice, though ever so justly offered.' The judgment on Athaliah is righteous — Henry does not soften it — but it must be executed outside the sanctuary. So the chapter's recurring question of the guard sounds once more (Levites, say Gill and Wesley; royal centurions, say Ellicott), yet the point that unites the voices is weightier than the point that divides them: even in the hour when the usurper is dragged to her death, the high priest will not let God's house be defiled, and the sentence of blood is carried beyond its gates.
16:
The sentence is carried out, and the witnesses fasten first on a fine ambiguity in how the guards handled her. The Reformed Gill gives both senses: 'they laid hands on her,' or 'rather,' with the Targum, Jarchi, and Kimchi, they 'set a place for her' — that is, 'made a space, opened a way for her to come out of the temple into their ranks, that she might be there taken and slain, and not in the temple.' The grammatical-historical commentators prefer this second reading. Keil follows the Chaldee, that they 'formed lines' and escorted her back; Ellicott sets the choice out plainly — the Greek and Latin have 'they laid hands on her,' but the Hebrew means 'they made room for her on both sides,' the crowd falling back so that 'a lane was formed for her exit.' So a real textual division stands: was she seized, or was she given passage through parted ranks? On the outcome there is no doubt.

The witnesses agree closely on the place of her death. Keil explains that she went 'by the way of the horses' entrance into the palace' and was there put to death, and he distinguishes this inner entrance for the royal mews from the horse-gate of the city wall named in Nehemiah. Clarke traces the same topography — they brought her out 'near the king's stables,' by the way the king's horses went to and from the stalls, the gate called in Chronicles 'the horse gate toward the king's house' (compare 2 Chron. 23:15). Ellicott sums it: 'Athaliah was conducted to the royal stables which adjoined the palace, and there put to death.'

The verse completes the great reversal, and the witnesses feel its justice without needing to preach it. She who had slaughtered the seed royal to seize the throne is led from the Temple she profaned and cut down at the horse-gate of the palace she usurped — not within the holy courts, which her blood must not defile, but at the very threshold of the house she had stolen. The place of her retreat becomes the place of her fall. The daughter of Jezebel dies as her mother died, and the throne of David, so long imperilled, is cleared at last of the usurper who sat upon it.
17:
With the usurper dead, Jehoiada lays the foundations of the new reign, and the witnesses read the double covenant as the pattern of a rightly ordered nation. The grammatical-historical Keil distinguishes the two compacts precisely: first, a covenant 'between the Lord on the one hand and the king and people on the other,' a renewal of the Mosaic covenant (compare Exodus 24; Deut. 4:20), by which both king and people bound themselves to live as the Lord's people and keep his law, grounded on 'the testimony' handed to the king; and second, a covenant 'between the king and the people,' by which the king bound himself to rule by God's law and the people to obey. The Reformed Gill reads it the same way — God renewing his pledge to be their God as they root out idolatry and restore his pure worship.

The Wesleyan voices divide the covenant on the same lines. Clarke calls the first 'a general covenant between the Lord, the Supreme King, the king his viceroy, and the people, that they should all be the Lord's people,' and the second 'a particular covenant' by which the king rules according to law and the people love, honour, and obey him — and where such just agreements are kept, 'there can be nothing else than prosperity in the Church and the state.' Wesley likewise names a sacred covenant to renounce all idolatry and maintain God's worship, and a civil covenant binding king and people to their mutual duties. Ellicott adds that the compact, violated by the recent Baal-worship, is here solemnly renewed (compare 1 Sam. 10:25), the king bound to the law of the testimony and the people to the house of David.

The puritan Matthew Henry gives the covenant its heart. Prince and people together in God's house 'covenant with God, and mutually covenant with each other,' and in this the king 'stands upon the same level with his subjects and is as much bound as any of them to serve the Lord.' He draws the order that makes all the difference: 'They first gave themselves to the Lord, and then to us' (2 Cor. 8:5), 'for by our bonds to God the bonds of every relation are strengthened.' The witnesses land as one: the deliverance is not secured by a crown alone but by a covenant, and a people is happy when its every change 'help[s] to revive, strengthen, and advance the interests of religion among them.'
18:
The covenant proves itself at once in action, and the witnesses read the demolition of Baal's house as the true fruit of renewed vows. The grammatical-historical Keil records the deed: all the people 'brake down the house of Baal, his altars and his images brake they in pieces thoroughly, and slew Mattan the priest of Baal before the altars,' after which 'the priest appointed officers over the house of the LORD.' The Reformed Gill supposes the temple of Baal to have been built by Athaliah in or near Jerusalem, perhaps on the mount that idolatry had made 'the mount of corruption.' The Wesleyan Clarke judges that Athaliah had set up Baal-worship in Judah as Jezebel had in Israel, or that it had lingered since Solomon's day, and points to the charge that 'the sons of Athaliah... had broken up the house of God' and lavished its dedicated things on the Baals (compare 2 Chron. 24:7).

The grammatical-historical Ellicott ties the demolition tightly to the covenant just made, 'of which the extirpation of the foreign Baal-worship was a consequence,' and thinks the house of Baal stood on the Temple mount 'in ostentatious rivalry with the sanctuary of Jehovah' (compare 2 Kings 21:4; 1 Kings 16:31). He explains the officers Jehoiada set over the house of the Lord as overseers appointed to prevent any future desecration (compare Ezek. 8:5) and to see the lawful worship duly carried out; and he notes that Mattan's very name, 'gift of Baal,' is a known Phoenician form.

The puritan Matthew Henry gives the scene its moral force. 'Pursuant to their covenant with God they immediately abolished idolatry'; the people pulled down Baal's temple, altars, and images, and though all his worshippers deserted him, his priest Mattan alone 'stuck to his altar,' and 'there he was slain, the best sacrifice that ever was offered upon that altar.' The witnesses do not quarrel except over the site of the fallen shrine — on the Temple mount, thinks Ellicott; on the mount of corruption, thinks Gill — and they agree on the lesson: a covenant with God that leaves the idols standing is no covenant, and the mark of a true reformation is that the altars of Baal come down and the worship of the Lord is restored.
19:
The reformation done, the king is led in triumph to his throne, and the witnesses trace the solemn procession from the house of God to the house of David. The Reformed Gill lists the escort — the rulers over hundreds, the captains, the guard, and all the people of the land who had gathered. The grammatical-historical Keil is careful with the verb: 'he took the captains' need not be pressed, for it means only that Jehoiada 'entrusted them with the duty of conducting the king into his palace,' the royal halberdiers who had gone over to the new king now following their captains; and so 'they brought the king down out of the house of Jehovah... and he sat on the throne of the kings.'

Ellicott maps the route in detail. Jehoiada 'arranges a procession to escort the king in triumph from the Temple to the palace': they 'brought down the king from the house of the Lord,' down from Moriah toward the bridge that joined the Temple hill to Zion, and 'entered the king's house by way of the gate of the Couriers' — a gate belonging, he insists, not to the Temple but to the palace, and probably its chief entrance. The proceedings end with the solemn enthronement, and here he prefers the reading of the Greek and of Chronicles, 'they seated him on the throne,' to the bare 'he sat.'

The puritan Matthew Henry gives the enthronement its dignity: the king was 'brought in state to the royal palace, and sat there on the throne of judgment, the thrones of the house of David,' ready to receive petitions and appeals, which in his childhood he would refer to Jehoiada. The witnesses read the verse as the completion of the day's great work. The heir who was crowned in the sanctuary is now installed in the palace; from the house of the Lord, where he had been hidden and guarded and crowned, he passes to the house of the king, where he is set upon the throne of his fathers. The circuit is complete: David's line, reduced to a hidden child, sits again upon David's throne, and the promise that had shrunk to a secret is now enthroned in the sight of the nation.
20:
The chapter draws to its close on joy and quiet, and the witnesses read in the people's gladness the vindication of God's promise. The Reformed Gill names the cause of the rejoicing: 'one of the house of David was set upon the throne, which they might fear was extinct,' for 'the lamp of David was almost quenched, only this single life left, from whom a line of kings proceeded, and the King Messiah'; and so, he concludes, 'the promise of God cannot fail.' The Wesleyan Clarke reads the peace of the city more simply — the people were 'glad to get rid of the tyranny of Athaliah,' and the city was quiet because 'she had no partisans to rise up and disturb the king's reign.'

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the wording. Keil records the plain sequence — 'all the people of the land rejoiced, and the city was in quiet: and they slew Athaliah with the sword beside the king's house.' Ellicott resists the critic Thenius, who would make 'all the people of the land' the exulting soldiery over against passive citizens; rather they are the countrymen and civilians, distinct from the royal guard, who 'accepted the revolution without attempting any counter movement.' He notes that the closing mention of Athaliah's death is best rendered 'Athaliah they had slain,' an emphatic backward glance to the real climax of the story (compare v. 16), and that 'beside the king's house' means within the palace enclosure.

The puritan Matthew Henry crowns the verse with its pastoral lesson. The people rejoiced and Jerusalem was quiet, and Josephus tells that they kept a feast of joy many days, so that here was fulfilled the word, 'When it goeth well with the righteous the city rejoiceth: and when the wicked perish, there is shouting' (Prov. 11:10). Henry draws the abiding point: 'The way for people to be joyful and at peace is to engage fully in the service of God.' The witnesses land together — the fall of the usurper and the enthroning of David's heir bring a joy that is more than political relief, for behind the shouting of the delivered city stands the faithfulness of the God who had sworn that David's lamp should never go out.
21:
The chapter ends where it began, on the single small life around which everything has turned. The Reformed Gill draws out the arithmetic the verse implies: Jehoash was 'seven years old... when he began to reign,' being 'a year old when his father was killed and having been hid six years in the temple.' The whole preceding drama is folded into that one number: one year of infancy, six years of concealment, and now a crown. The Wesleyan Clarke marvels at the rarity of it — 'the first instance on record of making a child seven years old the king of any nation, and especially of such a nation as the Jews, who were at all times very difficult to be governed.'

The grammatical-historical Ellicott adds only a note of arrangement: the Hebrew editions join this verse to the next chapter, where the reign of Joash is properly taken up (compare 2 Kings 12:1). The verse thus stands as a threshold, closing the account of the deliverance and opening the account of the reign it secured. Its very brevity is eloquent, for after the massacre, the hiding, the muster, the coronation, and the death of the queen, the story comes to rest on a boy of seven.

The witnesses feel the weight of that smallness. A seven-year-old king is no strength in himself; he had reigned only because he had first been hidden, and he had been hidden only because God would not let David's lamp go out. The child on the throne is the visible pledge of an invisible faithfulness: the line that had shrunk to one nursling in a Temple chamber is now enthroned, and through it runs the promise that reaches at last to the Son of David. What the chapter magnifies, in ending on so slight a figure, is not the king but the Keeper of the covenant — that against a queen's sword and six years of Baal, the word sworn to David held firm, and the crown came down, exactly as God had purposed, upon the head of a hidden child. The smaller the king, the plainer the grace; and the witnesses close the account as they opened it, wondering that so great a promise could rest so safely on so frail a life.
